title: 第 2 節
en: But where has Strauss or anybody else proved that true religion manifests itself in prayer, crossing, and attending mass only, and that all who do not pray, who do not cross themselves, and who do not THE PERCEPTION OF THE INFINITE. 3 attend mass, have no longer any religion at all, and no belief in God "? If we read on, we are almost tempted to admit that M. Renan was right in saying that those poor Germans try very hard to be irreligious and atheistical, but never succeed. Strauss says : ' The world is to us the workshop of the Rational and the Good. That on which we feel ourselves absolutely dependent is by no means a brute power, before which we must bow in silent resignation. It is order and law, reason and good ness, to which we surrender ourselves with loving confidence. In our inmost nature we feel a kinship between ourselves and that on which we depend. In our dependence we are free, and pride and humility, joy and resignation, are mingled together in our feeling for all that exists.'

If that is not religion, how is it to be called \ The whole argument of Strauss amounts, in fact, to this. He retains religion as the feeling of depen dence, in the full sense assigned to it by Schleiermacher, but he rejects the element added by Feuerbach, namely, the motive of covetousness, as both untrue, and unworthy of religion. Strauss himself is so completely in the dark as to the true essence of religion that when, at the end of the second chapter of his book, he asks himself whether he still has a religion, he can only answer, 'Yes^ or No, according as you understand it/ Yes, but this is the very point which ought to have been determined first, namely, what we ought to understand by religion. And here I answer that in order to understand what religion is, we must first of all see what it has been, and how it has come to be what it is.
Antiquity of Beligion.

Eeligion is not a new invention. It is, if not as old as the world, at least as old as the world we know. As soon almost as we know anything of the thoughts and feelings of man, we find him in possession of religion, or rather possessed by religion. The oldest literary documents are almost every where religious. ' Our earth/ as Herder1 says, tradition, whether literary or oral.' Even if we go beyond the age of literature, if we explore the deepest levels of human thought, we can discover, in the crude ore which was made to supply the earliest coins or counters of the human mind, the presence of religious ingredients. Before the Aryan languages separated — and who is to tell how many thousand years before the first hymn of the Veda or_the first line of Homer that ethnic schism may have happened? — there existed in them an ex pression for light, and from it, from the root div, • to shine, the adjective deva had been formed, meaning originally 'bright.' Afterwards this word deva was applied, as a comprehensive designation, to all the bright powers of the morning and the spring, as opposed to all the dark powers of the night and the winter : but when we meet with it O for the first time in the oldest literary documents, it is already so far removed from this its primitive etymological meaning, that in the Veda there are but few passages where we can with certainty 1 Herder, 'Ideen zur Geschichte der Menschheit,' 9. Buch, p. 130 (ed. Brockhaus).

THE PERCEPTION OF THE INFINITE. 5 translate it still by 'bright.' The bright dawn is addressed in the Veda as devi ushas, but it must remain doubtful whether the old poets still felt in that address the etymological meaning of bright- , ness, or whether we ought not to translate deva in the Veda, as deus in Latin, by God, however t^/^ difficult we may find it to connect any definite meaning with such a translation. Still, what we know for certain, is that deva came to mean ' god,' because it originally meant ' brighf,' and we cannot doubt that something beyond the meaning of bright- //, ness had attached itself to the word deva, before the ancestors of the Indians and Italians broke up from their common home.

Thus, whether we descend to the lowest roots of our own intellectual growth, or ascend to the loftiest heights of modern speculation, everywhere we find religion as a power that conquers, and conquers even those who think that they have conquered it.
Science of Religion.

Such a power did not escape the keen-eyed philosophers of ancient Greece. They, to whom the world of thought seems to have been as serene and transparent as the air which revealed the sea, the shore, and the sky of Athens, were startled at a very early time by the presence of religion, as by the appearance of a phantom which they could not explain. Here was the beginning of the science of religion, which is not, as has often been said, a science of to-day or of yesterday. The theory on the origin of religion put forward by Feuerbach in his work 'On the Essence of Christi- LECTURE I.

anity,' which sounds to us like the last note of modern despair, was anticipated more than two thousand years ago by the philosophers of Greece. With Feuerbach religion is a radical evil, inherent in mankind — the sick heart of man is the source of all religion, and of all misery. With Herakleitos, in the sixth century B.C., religion is a disease, though a sacred disease1. Such a saying, whatever we may think of its truth, shows, at all events, that religion and the origin of religious ideas had formed the subject of deep and anxious thought at the very beginning of what we call the history of philosophy.

I doubt, however, whether there was in the sayings of Herakleitos the same hostile spirit against all religion as that which pervades the writings of Feuerbach. The idea that to believe is meritorious, was not an ancient Greek idea, and therefore to doubt was not yet regarded as a crime, except where it interfered with public institutions. There was, no doubt, an orthodox party in Greece, 1 See 'Heracliti Ephesii Reliquiae,' ed. Bywater, p. 57, 1. 18, from 'Vita Heracliti e Diogene Laertio,' ix. i. Mr. Bywater places the saying rf)v re olrjcriv iepav vo<rov eXeye, among the Spuria, p. 51. It seems to me to have the full, massive and noble ring of Hera kleitos. It is true that oirja-is means rather opinion and prejudice in general than religious belief, but to the philosophical mind of Herakleitos the latter is a subdivision only of the former. Opinion in general might be called a disease, but hardly a sacred disease, nor can sacred disease be taken here either in the sense of great and fearful disease, or in the technical sense of epilepsy. If I am wrong, I share my error with one of the best Greek scholars and mythologists, for Welcker takes the words of Herakleitos in the same sense in which I have taken them. They are sometimes ascribed to Epikouros ; anyhow they belong to the oldest wisdom of Greece.
THE PERCEPTION OF THE INFINITE. 7 but we can hardly say that it was fanatical1 ; nay, it is extremely difficult to understand at what time it acquired its power and whence it took its coherence2.

Herakleitos certainly blames those who follow singers (aoifioi}3, and whose teacher is the crowd, who pray to idols, as if they were to gossip with the walls of houses, not knowing what gods and heroes really are. Epikouros does the same. But, unlike Epikouros, Herakleitos nowhere denies the J. , . — -- — *>*n existence of invisible Gods or of the One Divine. Only when he saw people believing in what the singers, such as Homer and Hesiod, told them about Zeus and Hera, about Hermes and Aphrodite, he seems to have marvelled; and the only explanation which he could find of so strange a phenomenon was that it arose from an affection of the mind, which the physician might try to heal, whensoever it showed itself, but which he could never hope to stamp out altogether.

In a certain sense, therefore, the science of religion is as little a modern invention as religion itself. Wherever there is human life, there is religion, and wherever there is religion, the question whence it came cannot be long suppressed. When children once begin to ask questions, they ask the why and the wherefore of everything, religion not excepted; nay, I believe that the first problems of what we call philosophy were suggested by religion.
It has sometimes been asked why Thales should be 1 Lange, ' Geschichte des Materialismus,' i. 4.
2 See E. Curtius, ' Uber die Bedeutung von Delphi fur die Griechische Cultur,' Festrede am 22 Februar, 1878.
8 ' Heracliti Reliquiae/ cxi., cxxvi.

called a philosopher, and should keep his place on the first page of every history of philosophy. Many a schoolboy may have wondered why to say that water was the beginning of all things, should be called philosophy. And yet, childish as that saying may sound to us, it was anything but childish at the time of Thales. It was the first bold denial that the gods had made the world ; it was the first open pro test against the religion of the crowd — a protest that had to be repeated again and again before the Greeks could be convinced that such thinkers as Herakleitos (Reliquise, xx) and Xenophanes had at least as good a right to speak of the gods or of God as Homer and other itinerant singers.

No doubt, at that early time, what was alone im portant was to show that what was believed by the crowd was purely fanciful. To ask how those fanciful opinions of the crowd had arisen, was a problem be longing to a later age. Still, even that problem was not entirely absent from the minds of the earliest thinkers of Greece ; for no one could have given the answer ascribed to Herakleitos, who had not asked himself the question which we ask ourselves to-day : What, then, is the origin of religion ? or, to put it into more modern language, How is it that we believe, that we accept what, as we are told by enemy and friend, cannot be supplied to us by our senses or established by our reason \ Difference between Ancient and Modern Belief.

It may be said that, when Herakleitos pondered on ofyo-iy, or belief, he meant something very different from what we mean by religion. No doubt he did ; for if there is a word that has changed from century THE PERCEPTION OF THE INFINITE. 9 to century, and has a different aspect in every country in which it is used — nay, which conveys peculiar shades of meaning, as it is used by every man, woman, or child — it is religion. In our ordinary language we use religion in at least three different senses : first, as the object of belief ; secondly, as the power of belief; thirdly, as the manifestation of belief, whether in acts of worship or in acts of real piety.

The same uncertainty prevails in other languages. It would be difficult to translate our word religion into Greek or Sanskrit ; nay, even in Latin, religio does by no means cover all that religion comprehends in English. We need not be surprised, therefore, at the frequent misunderstandings, and consequent wranglings, between those who write on religion, without at least having made so much clear to themselves and others, whether by religion they mean religious dogma, religious faith, or religious acts.

I have dwelt on this point in order to show you that it is not from mere pedantry if, at the very outset of these lectures, I insist on the necessity of giving a definition of religion, before we attempt another step in our journey that is to lead us as near as possible to the hidden sources of our faith.
Definitions of Religion.

It was, I think, a very good old custom never to enter upon the discussion of any scientific problem, without giving beforehand definitions of the principal terms that had to be employed. A book on logic or grammar generally opened with the question, What is logic 1 What is grammar ? No one would write on o o minerals without first explaining what he meant by 10 LECTURE I.

a mineral, or on art without defining, as well as lie might, his idea of art. No doubt it was often as troublesome for the author to give such preliminary definitions, as it seemed useless to the reader, who was generally quite incapable of appreciating in the beginning then: full value. Thus it happened that the rule of giving verbal definitions came to be looked upon after a time as useless and obsolete. Some authors actually took credit for no longer giving these verbal definitions, and it soon became the fashion to say that the only true and complete definition of what was meant by logic or grammar, by law or religion, was contained in the books them selves which treated of these subjects.

But what has been the result \ Endless misunder standings and controversies, which might have been avoided in many cases, if both sides had clearly defined what they did, and what they did not under stand by certain words.

With regard to religion, it is no doubt extremely difficult to give a definition. The word rose to the surface thousands of years ago ; it was retained while what was meant by it went on changing from century to century, and it is now often applied to the very opposite of what it was originally intended to signify.
Etymological Meaning of Religio.

It is useless with words of this kind to appeal to their etymological meaning. The etymological mean ing of a word is always extremely important, both psychologically and historically, because it indicates the exact point from which certain ideas started. But to know the small source of a river is very THE PERCEPTION OF THE INFINITE. 11 different from knowing the whole course of it : and to know the etymology of a word is very different from being able to trace it through all the eddies and cataracts through which it has been tossed and tumbled, before it became what it is now.

Besides, as with rivers, so with words, it is by no means easy to put our finger on the exact spot from whence they bubble forth. The Romans themselves felt doubtful as to the original meaning of religio. Cicero, as is well known, derived it from re-legere, to gather up again, to take up, to consider, to ponder — opposed to nec-ligere, to neglect ; while others derived it from re-ligare, to fasten, to hold back. I believe myself that Cicero's etymology is the right one ; but if religio1 meant originally attention, regard, 1 Religio, if it was derived from re-legere, would have meant originally gathering again, taking up again, considering carefully. Thus dl-ligo meant originally to gather, to take up from among other things ; then to esteem, to love. Negligo (nee-lego) meant not to take up, to leave unnoticed, to neglect. Intelligo meant to gather together with other things, to connect together, to arrange, classify, understand.

Relego occurs in the sense of taking back, gathering up (Ovid, Met. 8. 173) : Janua difficilis filo est inventa relecto, 'The difficult door was found by the thread [of Ariadne], which was gathered up again.' It is frequently used in the sense of travelling over the same ground : Egressi relegunt campos (Val. Fl. 8. 121). In this meaning Cicero thinks that it was used, when applied to religion : Qui omnia qua? ad cultum deorum pertinerent diligenter retractarent et tamquam relegerent, sunt dicti religiosi ex relegendo, ut eleganter ex eligendo, tamquam a diligendo diligenter, ex intelligendo intelligenter : his enim in verbis omnibus inest vis legendi eadem quse in religioso (Cic. de Nat. Deor. 2, 28, 72), ' People were called religious from relegere, because they went over again, as it were, and reconsidered carefully whatever referred to the worship of gods. '
zh: 但史特勞斯或其他任何人，何曾證明真正的宗教僅表現於祈禱、劃十字與參加彌撒，而所有不祈禱、不劃十字、不參加彌撒的人，便全然沒有宗教，也不信上帝？**無限者的感知**。3 若繼續讀下去，我們幾乎不得不承認，勒南先生說得對：那些可憐的德國人極力想成為無宗教與無神論者，卻從未成功。史特勞斯說：「對我們而言，世界是理性與良善的工坊。我們感到絕對依賴的，絕非一種野蠻的力量，我們必須在沉默的順從中向它低頭。那是秩序與法則、理性與良善，我們以信賴的愛心向它委身。在我們最內在的本性中，我們感到自己與所依賴者之間有一種親緣關係。在依賴中我們是自由的，而驕傲與謙卑、喜樂與順從，在我們對一切存在者的情感中交織在一起。」

如果那不算宗教，那又該如何稱呼？事實上，史特勞斯（Strauss）的全部論證歸結於此：他保留了宗教作為依賴感（the feeling of dependence）——此概念完全承襲施萊爾馬赫（Schleiermacher）所賦予的完整意涵——但他拒絕了費爾巴哈（Feuerbach）所添加的元素，亦即貪婪的動機（the motive of covetousness），認為它既不真實，也有損宗教的尊嚴。史特勞斯本人對於宗教的真正本質如此茫然，以至於當他在其著作第二章結尾自問是否仍擁有宗教時，只能回答：「是或否，端看你如何理解。」沒錯，但這正是首先應該確定的關鍵點，亦即我們應當如何理解宗教。而我在此的回答是：為了理解宗教是什麼，我們必須首先看清它曾經是什麼，以及它如何成為現在這個樣子。

我們習慣於將宗教視為人類心靈中一種幾乎與生俱來的稟賦，然而，當我們追溯其歷史源頭時，卻發現它並非始終如一。在人類意識的黎明時期，宗教的形態與我們今日所理解的截然不同。那些最古老的文獻，如《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中的讚歌，向我們展示了一個充滿生機與疑問的世界，其中對「無限者」（the Infinite）的追索，往往以一種樸素而直接的方式表達出來。這些早期的頌詩並非系統性的神學論述，而是人類面對浩瀚宇宙時，內心敬畏與好奇的自然流露。它們見證了宗教情感在尚未被教條與儀式固化之前的原始活力。因此，要理解宗教的本質，我們必須首先回到這些源頭，去傾聽那些來自遠古的聲音，它們或許能為我們揭示宗教之所以為宗教的永恆奧秘。

宗教並非一項新發明。它即便不與世界同壽，至少也與我們所知的世界一樣古老。幾乎在我們對人類的思想與情感有任何認識之際，便發現人已擁有宗教，或者更確切地說，是被宗教所擁有。最古老的文學文獻幾乎無一例外地帶有宗教性質。正如赫爾德¹所言：「我們的大地，無論是文學傳統還是口頭傳統，皆如此。」即便我們超越文學時代，深入探索人類思想的最深層，也能在那些用以鑄造人類心智最早錢幣或籌碼的粗礪礦石中，發現宗教成分的存在。在雅利安諸語言尚未分化之前——誰能說清，這種族群分裂可能發生在吠陀第一首讚歌或荷馬第一行詩句之前數千年？——其中已存在一個表示「光」的詞彙，並由此詞根 *div*（照耀）衍生出形容詞 *deva*，原意為「明亮的」。此後，這個詞 *deva* 被用作一個概括性的稱謂，指稱所有晨光與春日的光明力量，以對抗所有黑夜與寒冬的黑暗力量；然而，當我們在最早的文學文獻中首次遇見它時，它已遠離了其原始的字源意義，以至於在《梨俱吠陀》中，僅有少數段落我們能確切地…… ¹ 赫爾德，《人類歷史哲學理念》，第九卷，第130頁（布羅克豪斯版）。

5 仍將其譯為「明亮」。梨俱吠陀中以 *devi ushas*（明亮之黎明）稱呼破曉，但古代詩人在此稱呼中是否仍感受到「明亮」的詞源義，抑或我們應將吠陀中的 *deva* 比照拉丁文 *deus* 譯為「神」——儘管我們可能難以賦予此譯法任何明確意義——這點仍存疑。然而，我們確知的是：*deva* 之所以意指「神」，正因其原義為「明亮」；而我們無從懷疑，在印度人與義大利人的祖先從共同家園分道揚鑣之前，某種超越「明亮」之義的內涵已附著於 *deva* 一詞。
因此，無論我們是下探自身智識發展的最深根源，抑或上溯至現代思辨的最高峰，處處皆見宗教作為一種征服的力量，甚至征服了那些自以為已將其征服的人。
「宗教學」（Science of Religion）這個名稱，若就其最廣泛的意義而言，或許可被定義為一種對宗教現象進行科學探究的嘗試；也就是說，它旨在將人類心靈中那些被我們稱為宗教的種種直覺與能力，置於與其他科學門類相同的觀察、分類與比較法則之下。
這種力量並未逃過古希臘敏銳哲學家的眼睛。對他們而言，思想的世界如同雅典那顯露海洋、海岸與天空的空氣般澄澈透明，但他們在極早的時代便因宗教的存在而驚駭，彷彿見到無法解釋的幽靈現身。此即宗教學的開端——這門學科並非如常人所言，是今日或昨日才誕生的科學。費爾巴哈在其著作《論基督教的本質》中提出的宗教起源理論……（第一講）
「無神論」（anity）——在我們聽來宛如現代絕望的最後音符——早在兩千多年前，便已由希臘哲學家預示。對費爾巴哈而言，宗教是人類與生俱來的根本之惡：人類病態的心靈，是一切宗教與一切苦難的源頭。而在西元前六世紀的赫拉克利特看來，宗教是一種疾病，儘管是神聖的疾病¹。無論我們如何看待此說的真確性，這類言論至少表明：在我們所謂哲學史的濫觴之際，宗教與宗教觀唸的起源，早已成為深刻而焦慮的思辨主題。

我懷疑，赫拉克利特（Herakleitos）的言論中，是否帶有與費爾巴哈（Feuerbach）著作中那種瀰漫的、對一切宗教的敵意。認為「相信」是值得讚許的，這並非古希臘的觀念，因此「懷疑」也尚未被視為一種罪行，除非它幹擾到公共制度。希臘無疑存在一個正統派別¹。拜瓦特（Bywater）先生將「τήν τε οἴησιν ἱερὰν νόσον ἔλεγε」（他把意見稱為神聖的疾病）這句話歸入偽作（Spuria）之列，頁51。在我看來，這句話具有赫拉克利特那種飽滿、厚重且高貴的風格。誠然，οἴησις 的意義更偏向一般的意見與偏見，而非宗教信仰；但對於赫拉克利特的哲學心靈而言，後者僅是前者的一個分支。一般的意見或許可稱為一種疾病，但很難說是「神聖的疾病」；此處的「神聖的疾病」既不能理解為「重大而可畏的疾病」，也不能取其醫學上的專門意義「癲癇」。若我錯了，那麼我與一位最優秀的希臘學者暨神話學家共享此誤，因為韋爾克（Welcker）對赫拉克利特這句話的理解，與我相同。這句話有時被歸於伊壁鳩魯（Epikouros）；無論如何，它屬於希臘最古老的智慧。 ¹ 參見《赫拉克利特殘篇》（Heracliti Ephesii Reliquiae），拜瓦特編，頁57，第18行，引自《赫拉克利特傳》（Vita Heracliti e Diogene Laertio），ix. i。
7 但我們很難說那是狂熱¹；不，要理解它在何時獲得力量、從何處取得其連貫性²，是極其困難的。

赫拉克利特（Herakleitos）確實責備那些追隨吟遊詩人（ἀοιδοί）的人，他們以群眾為師，向偶像祈禱，彷彿在與房屋的牆壁閒談，卻不曉得神靈與英雄的真實本質。伊比鳩魯（Epikouros）也是如此。但與伊比鳩魯不同，赫拉克利特從未否認不可見之神或獨一神聖者的存在。唯有當他看見人們相信荷馬（Homer）與赫西俄德（Hesiod）這類吟遊詩人所講述的——關於宙斯（Zeus）與赫拉（Hera）、關於赫爾墨斯（Hermes）與阿佛洛狄忒（Aphrodite）——他似乎感到驚異；而他對如此奇異現象所能找到的唯一解釋是：這源於心智的一種病態（affection of the mind），醫師或許能在其顯現時試圖醫治，卻永遠無法指望將其徹底根除。
因此，在某種意義上，宗教學本身與宗教一樣，絕非現代的發明。凡有人類生命之處，便有宗教；凡有宗教之處，關於其源起的問題便無法長久壓抑。當孩童一旦開始發問，他們便會追問萬事萬物的「為何」與「緣由」，宗教亦不例外；甚至，我相信我們所謂哲學的最初問題，正是由宗教所啟發。
有時人們會問，為何泰勒斯（Thales）應被視為……¹ ¹ 朗格，《唯物主義史》，第一卷，第4頁。
2 參見 E. 庫爾提烏斯（E. Curtius）〈論德爾斐對希臘文化之意義〉（'Uber die Bedeutung von Delphi fur die Griechische Cultur'），1878 年 2 月 22 日慶典演說。
8 ‘ Heracliti Reliquiae/ cxi., cxxvi.

被稱為哲學家，並且理應在每一部哲學史的開篇佔有一席之地。許多學童或許曾納悶，為何說「水是萬物之源」這樣的話，竟能被稱為哲學。然而，儘管這句話在我們聽來可能顯得幼稚，但在泰勒斯（Thales）的時代，它絕非兒戲。這是第一次大膽地否認眾神創造了世界；這是第一次公開反對群眾宗教的抗議——這種抗議必須一再重複，希臘人才能相信，像赫拉克利特（Herakleitos，《殘篇》，xx）和克塞諾芬尼（Xenophanes）這樣的思想家，至少與荷馬（Homer）及其他遊吟詩人一樣，有權利談論諸神或上帝。

無疑地，在那個早期時代，唯一重要的，是向眾人展示：群眾所相信的，純屬虛幻。至於追問群眾那些虛幻的意見是如何產生的，這問題則屬於後世。儘管如此，即便是這個問題，也並未完全脫離希臘最早一批思想家的心靈；因為，若非有人曾向自己提出我們今日所追問的問題，便不可能給出歸於赫拉克利特（Herakleitos）的那個答案。那個問題便是：那麼，宗教的起源究竟是什麼？或者，用更現代的語言來說：我們如何會相信、如何會接受那些——正如敵友雙方都告訴我們的——既無法由感官提供、也無法由理性確立的東西呢？

可以說，當赫拉克利特（Herakleitos）思索 *πίστις*（或信念）時，他所指的與我們所謂的宗教大相逕庭。無疑確實如此；因為若有一個詞彙會隨著世紀更迭而改變，並在其使用的每個國度呈現不同面貌——甚至當它被每個男人、女人或孩子使用時，都傳達出獨特的意義層次——那便是「宗教」。在我們的日常語言中，我們至少在三個不同的意義上使用「宗教」：第一，作為信仰的對象；第二，作為信仰的力量；第三，作為信仰的表現，無論是在崇拜行為中，還是在真正的虔敬行為中。
同樣的不確定性也存在於其他語言中。要將我們所謂的「宗教」一詞翻譯成希臘文或梵文，將會相當困難；不僅如此，即使在拉丁文中，*religio* 也絕無法涵蓋英文中「religion」所包含的全部意涵。因此，我們無須訝異於那些撰寫宗教論述的人之間頻繁出現的誤解，以及隨之而來的爭論——他們至少未能向自己或他人釐清，所謂的「宗教」究竟指的是宗教教義、宗教信仰，還是宗教行為。
我在這點上著墨，是為了向各位表明：在本系列講座一開始，我便堅持必須先為宗教下定義，這並非出於迂腐之舉，而是因為在我們踏上旅程、試圖盡可能接近信仰隱藏源頭之前，這一步不可或缺。

在我們能夠明智地探討宗教學（the science of religion）之前，我們必須先對「宗教」一詞的涵義達成某種共識。然而，任何嘗試為宗教下定義的人，很快就會發現這項任務的困難所在。宗教的定義幾乎與宗教本身一樣繁多；而每一種定義，往往都帶有提出該定義者的個人信念或哲學偏見的印記。有些人將宗教定義為對神靈的信仰；另一些人則認為它是對無限者（the Infinite）的感知；還有人主張宗教本質上是一種情感，一種絕對依賴的感受；更有人將其視為一種道德規範，或一套儀式與實踐的體系。顯而易見，若我們要建立一門關於宗教的科學，我們就必須找到一個足夠寬泛的定義，能夠涵蓋所有被稱為宗教的現象，從最原始的拜物教形式，到最崇高的倫理一神教（monotheism）。這個定義不應預設任何特定的神學立場，而應純粹基於可觀察的事實。因此，我提議將宗教定義為：一種在心智中運作的能力或稟賦，無論其發展程度如何，都能使人領會那被感知為無限者（the Infinite）的存在——也就是說，領會那些超越感官與理性所能直接把握的事物。這個定義或許看似抽象，但它具有將宗教現象與其他所有人類活動區分開來的優點，同時也為宗教的歷史發展保留了充分的空間。
我認為，在討論任何科學問題之前，若不事先對所使用的主要術語加以定義，這向來是一項極好的古老習俗。一本關於邏輯或語法的書籍，通常會以「什麼是邏輯？」、「什麼是語法？」這樣的問題開場。沒有人會在撰寫礦物學著作時，不先解釋他所謂的「礦物」究竟意指為何。

若未先對「藝術」一詞加以界定，便無法撰寫礦物學或藝術論著，作者亦當盡其所能釐清自身對藝術的理解。無疑地，對作者而言，提供這類初步定義往往費時費力，而對讀者來說，此舉卻顯得徒勞無益——因為讀者起初通常完全無法體會這些定義的完整價值。正因如此，經過一段時間後，提供語詞定義的規則便被視為無用且過時。有些作者甚至以不再提供這類語詞定義而自詡，不久後便蔚為風尚：人們開始主張，所謂邏輯或文法、法律或宗教的真正完整定義，其實就蘊含在探討這些主題的著作本身之中。
但結果如何？無盡的誤解與爭論。在許多情況下，倘若雙方都能清楚界定他們對某些詞語的理解與不理解之處，這些爭論本可避免。
關於宗教，要給出一個定義無疑極其困難。這個詞在數千年前浮出水面；它被保留下來，而其所指涉的內涵卻隨著世紀更迭不斷變化，如今它常被用來指稱與其最初意圖表達的意義完全相反的事物。

「宗教」（religio）這個詞的語源學意義，長久以來一直是學者們爭論不休的議題。西塞羅（Cicero）將它與 *relegere*（重新審視、細心考量）聯繫起來，從而將宗教理解為一種謹慎的、反覆的敬拜實踐。另一方面，拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）則將其追溯至 *religare*（重新束縛、連結），強調人與神之間的一種連結或束縛。聖奧古斯丁（St. Augustine）也採納了後者的觀點。然而，現代語源學研究傾向於支持西塞羅的解釋，認為 *religio* 源自 *relegere*，意指一種在神聖事務上的審慎與敬畏，而非一種外在的束縛。這種內在的、反思性的態度，與我們在比較神學（comparative theology）中所探討的、人類對無限者（the Infinite）的普遍感知，有著深刻的共鳴。
對於這類詞彙，訴諸其字源意義是徒勞無功的。一個詞的字源意義，無論從心理學或歷史學角度來看，始終極其重要，因為它標示出特定觀唸的起點。然而，知道一條河流的微小源頭，與**知曉其完整流域**截然不同；同樣地，知道一個詞的字源，也與能夠追溯它在成為今日樣貌之前，歷經所有漩渦與瀑布的翻騰跌宕過程，大相逕庭。

此外，如同河流一般，語言的源頭也絕非易於精確指出其湧現之處。羅馬人自身對「religio」一詞的本義亦感猶疑。眾所周知，西塞羅將其追溯自「re-legere」，意為「重新收集、拿起、思考、深思」——與「nec-ligere」（忽略）相對；而另一些人則將其追溯自「re-ligare」，意為「繫緊、約束」。我個人認為西塞羅的詞源說法是正確的；然而，若「religio」¹ 原意為「專注、顧念」，¹ Religio，若源自 re-legere，則原意應為「重新收集、再次拿起、仔細考量」。因此，dī-ligo 原意為「收集、從眾物中揀選」；進而引申為「尊重、喜愛」。Negligo（nec-lego）意為「不拿起、不予注意、忽略」。Intelligo 意為「與其他事物一同收集、連結起來、整理、分類、理解」。

Relego 一詞具有「收回、重新收集」的含義（奧維德，《變形記》8.173）：*Janua difficilis filo est inventa relecto*，「那扇難以開啟的門，是靠著（阿里阿德涅的）線索才被找到的，而那條線索又被重新收攏起來。」此詞亦常用於表示「重新走過同一片土地」：*Egressi relegunt campos*（瓦萊裡烏斯‧弗拉庫斯，《阿爾戈船英雄記》8.121）。西塞羅認為，當這個詞被應用於宗教時，正是取此含義：「凡是仔細重新審視並彷彿重新檢視（relegerent）一切與諸神崇拜相關之事的人，便被稱為『虔誠者』（religiosi），這個詞源自『重新審視』（relegendo），正如『優雅者』（elegantes）源自『選擇』（eligendo），『勤勉者』（diligentes）源自『珍視』（diligendo），『明智者』（intelligentes）源自『理解』（intelligendo）；因為在這些詞語中，都蘊含著與『虔誠者』（religioso）相同的『審視』（legendi）意涵。」（西塞羅，《論諸神本性》2.28.72）
